question: A hotel has 144 rooms numbered 1 through 144. At the beginning of the day, every room is messy. There are 144 cleaning supervisors, also numbered 1 through 144. Supervisor 1 walks through every room and reverses its condition: messy rooms become clean, and clean rooms become messy. Supervisor 2 does the same for every room whose number is a multiple of 2. Supervisor 3 does the same for every room whose number is a multiple of 3. This continues until Supervisor 144 has finished. At the end of the process, is Room 96 clean or messy? How many rooms are clean?
answer: Room 96 is messy. There are 12 clean rooms.

question: A risk team is testing a fragile trading algorithm under increasingly severe market-stress scenarios numbered 1 through 100. There is an unknown threshold scenario N. If the algorithm is tested at scenario N or any more severe scenario, that copy of the algorithm becomes permanently corrupted. If it is tested below scenario N, it survives and can be tested again. The team has exactly 2 identical copies of the algorithm. They need to determine N with the smallest possible worst-case number of tests. What is the minimum worst-case number of tests, and what testing strategy should they use?
answer: 14 tests in the worst case. Use decreasing interval sizes 14, 13, 12, 11, ... . Test the first copy at scenarios 14, 27, 39, 50, 60, 69, 77, 84, 90, 95, 99, and 100. If the first copy becomes corrupted, use the second copy to search linearly between the previous safe scenario and the failure scenario.

question: Three trading algorithms, A, B, and C, are competing in a knockout stress-test tournament. They act in a fixed order: A, then B, then C, then back to A, continuing until only one algorithm remains. On its turn, each algorithm may challenge one remaining opponent or intentionally make no effective challenge. If the challenge succeeds, that opponent is removed from the tournament; if it fails, nothing happens. Algorithm A succeeds with probability 1/3, B succeeds with probability 2/3, and C succeeds with probability 1. All algorithms are strategic and want to maximize their own chance of being the final survivor. On A's first turn, should A challenge B, challenge C, or intentionally make no effective challenge? What is A's probability of ultimately winning under its best strategy?
answer: A should intentionally make no effective challenge on the first turn. Its probability of ultimately winning is 25/63, approximately 39.7%.

question: A batch-processing system labels trades using positive integer IDs. A particular trade ID must satisfy three audit rules. Rule 1: when the ID is divided by 4, it leaves remainder 3. Rule 2: when the ID is divided by 6, it leaves remainder 5. Rule 3: when the ID is divided by 9, it leaves remainder 7. Does such a positive trade ID exist?
answer: No such positive trade ID exists

question: A financial firm has N automated risk monitors. More than half of the monitors are reliable, and the rest are compromised. A reliable monitor always reports the true status of every other monitor. A compromised monitor may answer arbitrarily. You may ask a query of the form: 'Monitor i, is monitor j reliable?' What is the fewest number of queries needed in the worst case to identify one reliable monitor with certainty?

A trading platform processes user signups one by one. Each user is independently assigned one of 400 equally likely risk-profile codes. The platform gives a reward to the first user whose risk-profile code matches the code of any earlier user. You may choose your position in the signup queue before any codes are assigned. Which position should you choose to maximize your chance of receiving the reward?

A distribution center receives 205,891,132,094,689 crates each morning, labeled C000000000000001 through C205891132094689 in arrival order, forming a single intake queue. Note that 205,891,132,094,689 = 3^30 + 40. The supervisor walks down the queue inspecting crates in a repeating three-crate cycle: the first crate is cleared and sent to the warehouse floor; the second crate has a damaged seal and is sent to returns; the third crate has a labeling error and is sent for relabeling. The supervisor then repeats this same clear/return/relabel cycle. When the supervisor reaches the end of the queue, the cleared crates are rolled back onto the dock in the order they were cleared, rejoining the end of the queue for another pass. Returned and relabeled crates do not come back. The supervisor continues without pause into the returning batch, so the clear/return/relabel cycle does not restart at the beginning of a new pass. This continues until only one crate has not yet been sent to returns or relabeling. Which crate is it?

A compliance system stores 10 transaction files in chronological order, labeled F01 through F10. Exactly one file contains an error. You may inspect any file you choose. After each inspection, the system tells you whether the erroneous file is the inspected file, earlier than the inspected file, or later than the inspected file. What is the minimum number of inspections needed in the worst case to identify the erroneous file with certainty?

A database contains households with exactly two registered accounts. Each account is independently classified as Type A or Type B with equal probability. You are told that a randomly selected household has at least one Type A account. What is the probability that both accounts in that household are Type A?

Two data-cleaning scripts process transaction files. Script A can clean 1 file per hour. Script B can clean 2 files per hour. If both scripts work at the same time on the same batch, how long will it take them to clean 1 file-equivalent of work?
